Chemical potential.<[silence:30ms]> Energy on demand.<[silence:30ms]>

The word battery comes from "batterie," an old military term for grouped cannons—because early batteries stacked cells like cannons in a row, firing electricity instead of shots.<[silence:30ms]>

A battery is a chemical factory in a box.<[silence:30ms]> Inside, two metals (like zinc and copper) sit in a liquid or paste called an electrolyte.<[silence:30ms]> One metal wants to lose electrons;<[silence:40ms]> the other wants to gain them.<[silence:30ms]> But they can’t touch—so electrons race through a wire instead, powering your phone or flashlight.<[silence:30ms]> The reaction slows as chemicals deplete, like a soda going flat.<[silence:30ms]> Rechargeables reverse this:<[silence:40ms]> plugging in forces electrons backward, resetting the chemistry.<[silence:30ms]>

A car battery cranks 600 amperes to start an engine—enough to weld metal.<[silence:30ms]> A AA battery?<[silence:55ms]> Just 0.1 amperes, but lasts years in a remote.<[silence:30ms]>

clean_story:
Chemical potential. Energy on demand.

The word battery comes from "batterie," an old military term for grouped cannons—because early batteries stacked cells like cannons in a row, firing electricity instead of shots.

A battery is a chemical factory in a box. Inside, two metals (like zinc and copper) sit in a liquid or paste called an electrolyte. One metal wants to lose electrons; the other wants to gain them. But they can’t touch—so electrons race through a wire instead, powering your phone or flashlight. The reaction slows as chemicals deplete, like a soda going flat. Rechargeables reverse this: plugging in forces electrons backward, resetting the chemistry.

A car battery cranks 600 amperes to start an engine—enough to weld metal. A AA battery? Just 0.1 amperes, but lasts years in a remote.

Three related subjects are ion movement tricks, why batteries degrade, and energy without chemistry.
